notes: Probabilistic trigger propagation (P(lock)/P(violation) with the FIRE/ABORT_UNSAFE/HOLD operating point): a 64-sample approach trace (the per-shot planning call) and a 4096-sample campaign trace; Python and Rust/PyO3 runtime surfaces.

The FFI boundary is zero-copy in both directions (read-only NumPy views in, per-sample probability columns out as NumPy arrays); the per-call "batch" conversion pattern was measured first and lost the 4096-sample group to object-conversion overhead (134 ms vs 70 ms median) before the boundary was flipped to columns.

Both backends call the vendored fdlibm erfc (shared verbatim for bit-exact cross-backend parity; the platform erfc implementations differ by an ulp on real inputs). The pure-Python port makes the Python floor slower than the earlier math.erfc-based measurement; the floor pays for reproducibility, which is the documented trade.

Non-isolated local comparison run under taskset affinity and heavy host load (load average ~55-75); absolute timings are noisy and means are outlier-inflated. The fastest-first ranking (rust < python) holds in both groups across the distribution; do not treat as a production performance claim.
max_ns is recorded per surface; under this host load the max reflects scheduler preemption, not the kernel.

benchmark_context.isolation_method: taskset affinity to logical cores 8-11; no kernel isolcpus reservation
benchmark_context.heavy_jobs_running: shared workstation under heavy concurrent load (load average ~55-75); the rust<python ranking is robust to this load, absolute numbers are not.
benchmark_context.hardware_summary: x86_64, 12 logical CPUs, 6 cores, 2 threads per core
